inline_review:
commit 92cc3acd9e3943c91a2debaed01ced53fd96ffcc
Author: Li Wang <liwang@redhat.com>

selftests/mm: skip hugetlb_dio tests when DIO alignment is incompatible

This commit adds a check to query the filesystem's DIO alignment requirement
using statx(). It gracefully skips the hugetlb_dio test if the sub-page
offset used by the test is incompatible with the underlying filesystem's
block size.

> diff --git a/tools/testing/selftests/mm/hugetlb_dio.c b/tools/testing/selftests/mm/hugetlb_dio.c
> index 9ac62eb4c97d..afcca50d190e 100644
> --- a/tools/testing/selftests/mm/hugetlb_dio.c
> +++ b/tools/testing/selftests/mm/hugetlb_dio.c
> @@ -20,6 +20,31 @@
>  #include "vm_util.h"
>  #include "kselftest.h"
>  
> +#ifndef STATX_DIOALIGN
> +#define STATX_DIOALIGN		0x00002000U
> +#endif
> +
> +void check_dio_alignment(size_t pagesize)
> +{
> +	int fd;
> +	struct statx stx;

Since this file includes <sys/stat.h>, could this fail to compile on systems
with older glibc versions (pre-2.37) where struct statx lacks the
stx_dio_offset_align field?

Would including <linux/stat.h> or providing a local definition help support
older build environments?

> +	unsigned int dio_align = 1;
> +
> +	fd = open("/tmp", O_TMPFILE | O_RDWR, 0664);

Does this code accidentally turn a graceful test skip into a test failure
on filesystems that do not support O_DIRECT?

Previously, the test verified O_DIRECT support by opening the file with
O_TMPFILE | O_RDWR | O_DIRECT, gracefully skipping if it failed. By dropping
O_DIRECT here, open() might succeed, but the later open() in
run_dio_using_hugetlb() will fail and call ksft_exit_fail_perror() instead
of skipping.

> +	if (fd < 0)
> +		ksft_exit_skip("Unable to allocate file: %s\n", strerror(errno));
> +
> +	if (statx(fd, "", AT_EMPTY_PATH, STATX_DIOALIGN, &stx) == 0 &&
> +				(stx.stx_mask & STATX_DIOALIGN))
> +		dio_align = stx.stx_dio_offset_align;
> +
> +	close(fd);
> +
> +	if ((pagesize / 2) % dio_align != 0)

If statx() executes on a file or filesystem that does not support direct I/O,
the kernel sets stx_dio_offset_align to 0.

Since O_DIRECT was omitted from the open() above, the file might successfully
open but return a 0 alignment here. Could this result in a division by zero
crashing the test?

> +		ksft_exit_skip("DIO alignment (%u) incompatible with sub-page offset %lu\n",
> +				dio_align, pagesize / 2);

This isn't a bug, but using %lu for pagesize / 2 (which is a size_t) can
trigger a format compiler warning on 32-bit platforms. Should this use %zu
instead?

[ ... ]
output:
{"findings":[{"problem":"Missing O_DIRECT in check_dio_alignment() causes false test failure on filesystems lacking DIO support.","severity":"Medium","severity_explanation":"Before the patch, the test verified `O_DIRECT` support by opening `/tmp` with `O_TMPFILE | O_RDWR | O_DIRECT`, gracefully skipping the test if it failed. The patch removes `O_DIRECT` from this initial open. Consequently, if the filesystem supports `O_TMPFILE` but not `O_DIRECT`, `check_dio_alignment()` will pass. The test then proceeds to `run_dio_using_hugetlb()`, which attempts to open the file with `O_DIRECT`, fails, and calls `ksft_exit_fail_perror()`. This incorrectly turns a valid test skip into a test failure."},{"problem":"Division by zero when `statx` reports no direct I/O support.","severity":"Medium","severity_explanation":"According to the `statx(2)` manual and kernel implementation (e.g., ext4 with data journaling), if direct I/O is not supported on a file, `stx_dio_offset_align` is set to 0. Since `check_dio_alignment()` no longer opens the file with `O_DIRECT`, it can successfully open a file lacking DIO support, call `statx()`, and receive `stx_dio_offset_align == 0`. The subsequent modulo operation `(pagesize / 2) % dio_align` will result in a division by zero, crashing the selftest."},{"problem":"Build failure on systems with older glibc headers due to missing `stx_dio_offset_align` in `struct statx`.","severity":"Low","severity_explanation":"The patch provides a fallback definition for `STATX_DIOALIGN` but unconditionally accesses `stx.stx_dio_offset_align`. Because the test includes `<sys/stat.h>`, it relies on the glibc definition of `struct statx`. On older glibc versions (pre-2.37), `struct statx` lacks the `stx_dio_offset_align` field, causing the compilation to fail. Including `<linux/stat.h>` or providing a local struct definition is necessary to support older build environments."},{"problem":"Compiler warning due to format specifier mismatch for `size_t`.","severity":"Low","severity_explanation":"In the `ksft_exit_skip` call, the format string uses `%lu` to print `pagesize / 2`, which is of type `size_t`. On 32-bit platforms, `size_t` is typically an `unsigned int`, and using `%lu` will trigger a `-Wformat` warning. The correct format specifier for `size_t` is `%zu`."}],"fixes":"","review_inline":"commit 92cc3acd9e3943c91a2debaed01ced53fd96ffcc

> +	struct statx stx;

> +
